spec_match.rationale: Calls CSS.supports('at-rule(@x)') exactly as defined by css-conditional-5 §support-definition-at-rules — both via a dropdown / custom input and a 14-rule battery.

interactive.rationale: Dropdown, custom-input fallback, check button, Enter-to-submit, plus a battery of 14 chips that all probe live. Real CSS.supports() calls.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'pick an at-rule and see what CSS.supports("at-rule(@x)") returns in this browser, plus a battery of common at-rules tested in one shot' — page delivers exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: Catches the throw case (older browsers that don't parse `at-rule()`) with a guidance note; never demonstrates the spec's behaviour for prefixed names (`@-webkit-keyframes`), the case-insensitivity, or `at-rule()` combined with `and` / `or` operators.

design_system.rationale: All colours from CSS variables; pass/fail uses --accent-emerald / --accent-rose against --text-black, AA-clean.

openQuestions.title: Demo `at-rule()` inside compound @supports expressions
openQuestions.detail: The spec lets you AND/OR `at-rule()` with property queries: `@supports (display: grid) and at-rule(@container) { … }`. A second probe row that lets users build a compound expression and shows the truth table would teach the integration with @supports' existing operators.

openQuestions.title: Cover vendor-prefixed at-rules
openQuestions.detail: `@-webkit-keyframes` and `@-moz-document` still exist in legacy CSS. Add a probe row for at least one prefixed name so visitors learn whether the conditional matches them in their browser (the spec says yes if the prefixed rule is implemented).

openQuestions.title: Show the descriptor-form `at-rule(@x; prop: val)`
openQuestions.detail: css-conditional-5 also allows probing individual descriptors of an at-rule (e.g. `at-rule(@font-face; font-display: swap)`). The page only exercises the bare-name form. A second tab or section for descriptor probing would cover the spec's other half.

openQuestions.title: Add an `@scope` chip with a hover-tooltip explanation
openQuestions.detail: The battery list contains @scope but with no per-chip explanation; for less-known at-rules a single-sentence tooltip would help newcomers understand what they're probing. Currently you have to leave to the matrix page for descriptions.

summary: Tight CSS.supports('at-rule(...)') probe with a 14-rule battery — solid spec match on the bare-name form. Two real gaps: compound @supports expressions (and/or) and the descriptor-form `at-rule(@x; prop: val)` are both spec features the page never touches.
